exits.label: ◂ break camp — back to the Market of the Causeway
▴ on up the road — the Grey Wayshrine
◂ a lantern off the road — the Mapmaker's Wagon

npcs.name: the fire
npcs.title: a quiet hour — rest & take stock
npcs.hail: *(the fire has burned down to its steady heart, and the night is large and quiet around it, and for the first time in a long while there is nothing to do but sit, and breathe, and reckon up what you have done)*

npcs.name: Dace Iron
npcs.title: your companion, off-watch
npcs.hail: *(the sellsword sits a little apart from the fire, where the dark is, sword across her knees out of habit more than need, and for once she isn't pretending to sharpen it)*

npcs.name: Wren
npcs.title: alive, and at the fire
npcs.hail: *(the apple-girl sits cross-legged by the flames, turning a winter apple over and over in her hands without eating it, watching the sparks go up and not, this time, going up with them)*

npcs.name: Pip
npcs.title: warm, for once
npcs.hail: *(the street-child is asleep sitting up against a bedroll, or pretending to be, one eye cracking open to track you the instant your boot finds the camp — old habits — but her shoulders, for once, are down)*

npcs.name: Sennet
npcs.title: your cartographer, mapping the fire
npcs.hail: *(the cartographer has claimed a flat stone by the fire as a drawing-table and is, of course, working — but it's a different work tonight: not the country of the dead, but a small careful sketch of the camp itself, the fire and the bedrolls and the souls around it, each one labelled in the tiny deep-country hand, as though afraid of losing the bearings of this, too)*

conversations.title: A Quiet Hour by the Fire

variants.text: *(You sit, and the cold that lives in you since the Wall settles, as it always does, a half-degree deeper than the fire can reach. The flames warm your hands and stop at your wrists, the way the living warmth always does now. You have spent this whole long day among souls coming loose from the world, and carried a little of each of them out, and they sit with you now in the dark behind your eyes — Wren's apple, Wick's terms, Esuele's thimble, the old gravedigger's peace. The fire does not ask anything of you. It is the first thing all day that hasn't.)*

*(You sit, and for once the ledger of the day reads in the black. You went down a cold causeway full of people the world had already decided to throw away, and you spent yourself, soul by soul, refusing the arithmetic — and some of them are alive, or eased, or remembered, because of choices only you made. The fire pops. It is a small warmth in a large dark, which is, you are beginning to suspect, the only kind there has ever been, and the only kind worth tending.)*

*(You sit, and the day's work settles in you like a stone in cold water. You got things done. You frightened the frightening, you bent the unbending, you made the smiling men stop smiling — and if a few souls got spent to do it, well, the causeway was spending souls long before you arrived, and at least yours bought something. The fire throws your shadow long against the trees. It is a hard, clean warmth, and you have earned it, and you do not look too long at the dark past its edge.)*

*(You sit by the burned-down fire, and the night is large and quiet, and the day uncoils behind you — the market, the river, the bridge, the Wall, the Quarter, the counting-house. So much, in one turning of the light. You came to this causeway a stranger with the Wall's cold in your bones, and you are leaving it the thing the whole rotten machine has started, very quietly, to be afraid of. The fire burns steady. It asks nothing. Sit a while.)*

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(The flames burn low and steady, and the dark holds the camp gently, and there is — rarely, briefly — time to think.)*

choices.text: *(reckon up the souls at the river — Wren, and the boatman, and the drowned weaver)* "Take stock of the causeway."
*(reckon up the bridge and the Wall — the boy, the blind weaver, the soul in the stone)* "Take stock of the bridge and the Wall."
*(reckon up the Choir — the smiling man, the clerk, the Canon, and the crack you put in it all)* "Take stock of the Choir."
*(sit with what you are — the cold, the coming-back, the road still ahead)* "Take stock of yourself."
*(bank the fire, and rest)* "Enough reckoning. Sleep."

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(You think of the water, and the cold reed-smell of it.)* *(If Wren walks the world tonight instead of the causeway, it is because you stood in a market and made a frightened girl believe her fear was right; she greeted you at the river as a saviour, and the word still sits oddly on you, and rightly. If you sent her to the smiling man instead — that sits oddly too, and will, for longer. Old Cassian still rows his thirteen thousand, but he waits now on a question you planted, a reason for the trip to end somewhere he'll hear of. And the Reed-Wife abides in her green doorway, four centuries lonely, a little less so for an hour of being heard. The river takes everyone, in the end. You made it take a few of them gentler.)*

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(You think of the cold stone, and the faces in it.)* *(There is a boy under a bridge who may yet live, if the greywort you carried out of the marsh brews true — a cure fetched from the foot of the Wall itself, which has never in four hundred years given anything back. There is a blind weaver at her loom who knows, now, that someone heard the name she was drowned for. And there is Esuele in the stone, worn down to a thimbleful, holding on — because you carried her mother's love across a threshold the gods said love could not cross. The Wall is very large, and very old, and very patient. And you reached into it, twice, and pulled something back out. It will remember that. It has started to.)*

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(You think of the brass scales, and the quiet little books.)* *(You walked into the Choir's own counting-house and you cracked its High Measurer — a true believer, thirty years certain, now sitting at her great scale feeling the certainty spread like a flaw through glass. Whether you forced the souls' appeal, or sanctioned the cutting of Venn's signature, or simply made her audit her own ledger until it betrayed her, the machine that feeds the Wall has, for the first time in four centuries, a person inside it who has decided to make it stop. That is how the enormous things fall, in the end. Not all at once. One bookkeeper at a time, deciding the book was wrong.)*

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(You hold your hands to the flames, and watch the warmth stop at your wrists, where it always stops now.)* *(You died. You remember that much, and less than you'd like — the harvest, the cold, the stone closing over. And then something pulled you back, and you came up wearing the Wall like frost on a window, a soul that goes both ways through a door the cosmos built to swing only one. Every soul on this causeway felt it: the boatman, the Measurer, the clerk, the Canon — the cold thing that should be filed and isn't. You frighten them. You should. You are the counterexample that unmakes their rule, walking around on its own two legs, warming its dead hands at a living fire. The road ahead runs to a Crown, and to a man who broke the way you might yet break, and to other masks behind his. But that is tomorrow. Tonight there is the fire, and the cold in your wrists, and the strange new fact of being a thing the Wall is afraid of. Rest, Returned. You have earned the dark's good half.)*

nodes.speaker: the fire
nodes.text: *(You bank the coals, and lie back, and the great quiet night closes over the camp like water over a stone — but gently, this time, and not for keeps. Tomorrow the road. Tonight, the rare and ordinary miracle of a warm place to lie down, among — if you earned them — a few souls who are alive to share the dark. The fire keeps its small watch. Sleep.)*

variants.text: *(She doesn't reach for the whetstone when you sit — that's new — and the look she gives you across the dark has none of the market's wary edge left in it.)* …Couldn't sleep either, eh. *(she shifts to make room on the log, easy, like you've done this a hundred times)* Funny thing. Ten years I slept like a *stone* — in *barracks*, in *ditches*, in the *back rooms* of people I'd been paid to *frighten.* Slept *fine.* *(she watches the fire)* And now I've thrown in on something I actually *believe* in, and suddenly I can't close my eyes for *thinking* about it. *(a low, surprised laugh)* Turns out *caring* about the outcome's *terrible* for the *rest.* *(she glances at you, almost shy under the scars)* …I don't *regret* it. Want that *said.* Best decision I've made since I picked up the *sword.* I just — didn't know it'd come with *lying awake.* Sit. Keep a fool company. I find I'd rather not do the *thinking* alone.

*(She watches you approach and tips her chin at the log beside her, the wary professional distance of the Lamplit Quarter not quite gone yet, but thinning.)* …So. *(rasp of nothing; her hands are still for once)* This is the part nobody tells you about, when you throw in with a *cause.* The *quiet* part. The *fire* part. *(she looks at the flames)* On a *contract*, the night before, you sleep or you *drink* — there's *nothing* to think *about*, it's just *work.* But *this* — *(she gestures vaguely at you, the dark, the road)* — *this* I keep *turning over.* *(she eyes you, guarded)* You're a hard one to get the *measure* of, you know that? I've thrown my *whole* sword behind you and I still don't rightly know *what* you are. *(a beat)* …Sit, if you like. Maybe I'll work it out by *firelight.*

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(She leans back against the log, the fire painting the old scars gold, and for once she looks less like a blade and more like a person who happens to carry one.)* Go on, then. *(half a smile)* It's the *fire.* The fire's where the *real* talking happens — everyone knows that. *Daylight's* for *lying.* So. What d'you want to know about the sellsword you've staked your war on? Ask while I'm *soft* with the *quiet.* It won't last past *dawn.*

choices.text: "Why this fight, Dace? Truly. You turned down a year of Choir gold. A blade-for-hire doesn't starve on *principle* unless the principle costs them something specific. What did the Wall take from you?"

[INSIGHT] "You keep your sword across your knees even off-watch. That's not habit, that's a *wall* — you've been let down by everyone you ever served, and some part of you is braced for me to be the next one. Say it. I'd rather you said it than carried it."

*(kin to kin, cold to Faithless)* "You said the Wall's got a space shaped like you. I've been *in* that space, Dace — climbed out of it. So I'll make you a promise no employer ever could: whatever happens out there, I will not let that space have you. Not while I'm walking both sides of the door."
"Get some sleep, Dace. The road's long and I'd rather my sword-arm rested. We'll talk at dawn."

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(The half-smile fades. She's quiet a while, watching the fire, and when she speaks it's lower, and the rasp has gone out of it.)* …My sister. *(simply)* Younger than me. *Sweeter.* The kind of soul who gave her last copper to someone worse off and *meant* it — no god, mind, neither of us ever had the *coin* for one, but if *kindness* counted she'd have been a *saint.* *(her jaw works)* She got *sick.* The slow kind. And when she *went*, the Choir came with their *scale*, and they measured her *light* — *unbought*, *unvouched* — and they walked her out past the *last torch* while I was three *towns* over earning the coin that came too *late.* *(she looks at you)* I didn't even get to *bury* her. There's no *grave.* There's just — a *space.* In a *wall.* With my sister's *kindness* in it, dissolving into *nothing*, because no *god* happened to *want* her and I wasn't *there* to *fight.* *(fiercely, quietly)* So that's the fight, Returned. That's *the* fight. Every marked soul we pull off that causeway is *her*, a *little.* I can't get *her* back. *(a breath)* But you've shown me you can get *others* back. And I will spend every *swing* I've got making sure *no one else* comes home three towns *too late.*

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(She goes still. Looks down at the sword across her knees like she's seeing it for the first time. And then — slowly, deliberately — she lifts it and sets it down in the dirt *beside* her, out of her hands, and the gesture costs her something visible.)* …Yeah. *(rough)* Yeah. There it is. *(she flexes her empty hands)* Every soul I ever drew that blade for *sold* me, or *spent* me, or *ran* when it got *hard* and left me holding the *line* alone. You learn to keep the *sword* close and the *people* far, and you call it *professionalism*, and it *works* — *(she looks at you)* — it works right up until someone walks up off a cold causeway and asks you to believe in something, and you *realise* you've forgotten *how.* *(a shaky breath)* I'm *braced* for you to be the next one. You're *right.* Can't help it. It's *scar tissue.* *(she nudges the sword further away with her boot, a decision)* But I just put the *sword* down. First time since — gods, since my *sister.* *(she meets your eyes, raw)* That's me *trying*, Returned. Don't make me *regret* the *trying.* It's the bravest thing I've got, and I've stormed *breaches.*

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(She snorts and the sword stays exactly where it is, across her knees.)* *Wall*, is it. *(dryly)* It's a *sword*, friend. It goes on my *knees* because that's where *swords* go when you're *sitting.* Don't read me like one of your *causeway* souls — I'm not *dying*, I've no *tragic* glow, I'm just a *tired* sellsword who likes her *steel* where she can *reach* it. *(she eyes you, but there's no heat in it)* …Ask me something *easier*, eh? The *deep* stuff's for when I've known you longer than a *day.* Throw enough *fires* between us and *maybe* I'll do the *bleeding-heart* bit. *Maybe.* For now — *(she resettles)* — the sword stays. Habit's *earned.*

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(She turns to look at you full-on, and the firelight catches something wet in the scarred face that she does not bother, this time, to hide.)* …You can't *promise* that. *(quietly)* Nobody can promise that. The space in the Wall is *patient*, it gets *everyone*, it'll get *me* the day my *guard* drops a half-second too — *(she stops, because you're still looking at her, cold and certain and inarguably *back* from exactly the place she fears)* …except *you're* standing there having *done* the impossible thing. *(a cracked breath)* Climbed *out.* Walk *both* sides of the *door.* *(she scrubs her face, furious at the wet)* Gods *damn* you, Returned. Ten years I made my peace with that *space* — *my* sister's in one, I always *knew* I'd join her, it was the one *certainty* I had — and you've gone and put a *crack* in even *that.* *(she picks the sword back up, but holds it differently now, like a *tool* and not a *shield*)* …All right. *All right.* I'll hold you to it. The *impossible* promise. And I'll guard your *back* like it's *true*, because if *anyone* could make it true, it's the *cold bastard who already did it once.* *(fiercely)* Don't you *dare* go into that Wall without me. We're *both* getting out. *Both* of us. That's the *new* certainty. I *like* it better.

nodes.speaker: Dace Iron
nodes.text: *(She huffs a quiet laugh and finally, *finally* lets the sword tip sideways off her knees.)* …Resting the sword-arm. *(she shakes her head, almost fond)* Most employers want it *sharpening* till *midnight* and *up* before *dawn.* *(she settles back against the log)* You're a *strange* one, Returned. *(a yawn)* All right. *Dawn*, then. *(her eyes are already closing)* …First decent *sleep* I'll have had in a *month*, I reckon. On account of — *(she's drifting)* — on account of for once I *believe* the thing I'll *wake up* to. *(quietly, almost gone)* …Don't let the fire die. The *dark* out here's got *teeth.* And — *(barely)* — glad I threw in. Didn't say it *plain* yet. So. There. *Plain.*

variants.text: *(She's still got it — the grey holding-stone, Sable's stone, the one you pressed into her wet hands at the river. She's threaded it on a cord now, and it sits at her throat, and her fingers find it when she sees you.)* *(she smiles, and it's a real one, sized for a person and not for a seller)* I kept it. *(she touches the stone)* Course I kept it. You *trapped* me into living with it, remember — said you'd be the one who *remembers*, and then I couldn't very well go and *die* and leave you holding a stone you'd promised to — *(a wet laugh)* — anyway. It *worked.* The trap. I'm *here.* At a *fire.* Being *remembered*, and *doing* the *remembering* back. *(she shifts over)* Sit with me? I've spent a *week* learning how to sit somewhere and *not* be measuring the *distance* to the *water.* I'm getting *good* at it. Come see.

*(The apple-girl looks up from the flames, and the haunted edge that lived on her at the river is — not gone, but *quieter*, banked like the fire.)* *(she smiles)* There you are. *(she turns the winter apple over in her hands)* I keep not eating it. *(she laughs at herself)* Funny — at the water I couldn't stop *eating* them, each one like it might be the *last sweet thing.* And now I've got a whole *future* of apples and I keep — *saving* this one. *(she sets it down, deliberate)* Habits of the nearly-gone, I suppose. I'm still learning which ones to keep. *(she pats the ground beside her)* Sit? You're the reason there's a *future* to have habits *in.* The least I can do is share the *fire* of it.

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She sits in the firelight, alive, the cold river a day and a choice behind her, and looks at you with a frank warmth that still seems to surprise her.)* So. *(she tilts her head)* What does a saviour and their saved talk about, at a fire, when the saving's *done?* I haven't the *foggiest.* Nobody ever *saved* me before — I rather assumed the story just *ended* at the riverbank. *(a soft laugh)* But it *doesn't*, does it. There's all this — *after.* What do we *do* with the after?

choices.text: "You live in it, Wren. That's the whole of it. Loudly, inconveniently, eating every apple you like. The after is the part the Choir didn't think you'd get. Spend it like it's stolen — because it is, and you stole it back."

"What will you do now, Wren? Truly. The mark's still on you somewhere — but the causeway isn't your only road anymore. Where does a girl go, when she's decided to be inconvenient for as long as she can?"

[INSIGHT] "You're happy, and it's terrifying you. You spent so long braced for the end that being *safe* feels like a trick — like if you let yourself want the future, something will come and take it. Let me say it plainly so you don't have to carry it alone: it's real, Wren. You're allowed."

*(quietly, one marked soul to another)* "The mark's still on you. I can still feel it. I won't lie about that, Wren — I never have. But I've been past the last torch, and I'm working on the door, and I want you to know: I'm not done. Living *now* doesn't mean the Wall gets you *later.* I'm coming for the whole thing."
"Rest, Wren. You've earned the fire. We'll talk more on the road."

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She picks the apple back up, looks at it, and — finally, decisively — takes an enormous, dribbling, joyful bite, and talks around it, laughing.)* *Stole it back.* *(she chews, grinning)* Gods, that's — *yes.* That's *exactly* it, isn't it. They had me *measured.* *Filed.* *Spent.* And I just — *walked off the ledger.* *(she swallows)* Every breath I take now is a *breath they didn't budget for.* Every apple's a *theft.* *(fierce, delighted)* Then I'm going to be the *greatest thief on the causeway*, and I'm going to steal *years*, and I'm going to do it so *loud* and so *inconvenient* that somewhere a Measurer has to *cross my name out* and *grind her teeth.* *(she nudges you with a shoulder)* You did that. You made dying *embarrassing* and living *spiteful* and I have *never* been so *glad* to be *out of pocket* to anyone in my *life.* *(softer)* Thank you. I'll spend the whole *stolen* lot of it being worth the *theft.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She's quiet a moment, and something thoughtful and unafraid settles over her.)* …You know what I keep coming back to? *(she watches the fire)* All those others. At the river, on the causeway, in the dark — all the marked ones, with no one to stand in their market and make them *brave.* *(she looks at you)* I had *you.* It was *luck.* Dumb, blind *luck* that the one stranger who'd *bother* walked past my apple-barrel on the *right* morning. *(she sets her jaw)* That's not *good enough.* Nobody's *future* should hang on whether a *saviour* happens by. *(quietly fierce)* So I think — I think I want to be the one who *bothers.* For the *next* Wren. I know the river now. I know the cold water and the brave arithmetic and the *exact* lie the Choir tells. *(she meets your eyes)* I could *stand* at that water, where you stood for *me*, and I could *talk them back.* *(a breath)* You gave me a *future*, and it turns out the future I *want* is to *give it back.* Is that — is that a *daft* thing to do with a *stolen* life?

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(Her breath catches, and her hand goes to her mouth, and for a second the firelit composure cracks right down the middle.)* …*Allowed.* *(barely)* How did you — *(she presses the heel of her hand to one eye)* — I haven't *said* it. To *anyone.* That it feels like — like a *trick*, like *thin ice*, like the moment I *let* myself be *happy* the cold water comes *back* for being *cheated.* *(a shaky breath)* I keep *not* eating the apple because some *animal* part of me thinks if I *enjoy* it too much, that's when it gets *taken.* *(she looks at you, wet-eyed and trying to smile)* And you just — *said* it. *Out loud.* *'It's real. You're allowed.'* *(she laughs, broken and relieved)* Nobody's ever *given* me *permission* to be *all right* before. I didn't know I was *waiting* for it. *(she takes a breath, steadier)* …Okay. *(she picks up the apple)* Okay. *Allowed.* I'll — I'll *practise* it. The being-all-right. *(a real smile, finally)* You'll have to *remind* me. When I get that *braced* look. Will you? Just — say it again. *Allowed.* I think I'll need it more than *once.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She gives you a determined smile, a little too bright, and turns the apple over in her hands.)* I'm *fine.* *(she says it the way you say a thing you're *deciding* rather than *reporting*)* I'm *alive* and I'm *warm* and there's a *fire*, and that's — that's *everything*, isn't it. That's *plenty.* *(she nods, convincing herself)* Don't *fuss* over me. I did *enough* falling-apart at the *water* to last a *lifetime* — a *lifetime I've now got*, ha. *(the smile holds, just)* I'm *all right.* Really. *(quieter)* Or I *will* be. Same thing, near enough, if you *say* it firmly. *(she pats your hand)* Sit. Watch the fire. I'm *better* with company that doesn't *poke* at the *soft* bits. They're still *healing.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She doesn't flinch at the mention of the mark — she's past flinching — but her hand drifts to her sternum, where the cold of it lives, and her eyes find yours with a fierce, frightened trust.)* …I know it's still there. *(quietly)* I feel it. Cold spot, right *here*, like a coin of *winter* under the skin. Most days I don't *think* about it. Some nights — *(she shakes her head)* But you just said the thing I *needed* and *dreaded* in the same *breath.* That you're *not done.* That living *now* isn't the *whole* win — that you mean to go *back* and unmake the *thing* that *marked* me. *(she takes your cold hand in both of hers, like she did at the river, and doesn't flinch at the chill)* …Then let me say *my* part of it, marked soul to *Returned*: when you go for that Wall — and I can see you *will* — you carry *me* with you. Not the *person*; I'll be busy *living*, like you *ordered.* *(a fierce, wet grin)* But the *reason.* I'm the *proof* it's *worth* it. Every cold night the road gets *long* and you wonder if it *matters* — you *remember* there's a girl eating *apples* she *stole back*, because you *bothered.* *(she squeezes)* Now go *bother* the *Wall.* I'll keep the *fire* warm for when it's *done.*

nodes.speaker: Wren
nodes.text: *(She smiles and draws her knees up, settling toward the fire's warmth.)* The road. *(she tries the word out)* I've never *had* a road before — just the *market*, and the *river*, and the little circle between. *(she looks out at the dark, and for once it doesn't seem to frighten her)* A *road* means *somewhere else.* *Somewhere after.* *(softly)* I think I'm going to *like* having one. *(she yawns)* Rest *well*, you. *(as you turn)* And — hey. *(quietly, fierce)* Whatever you are, wherever that road *takes* you — there's a girl by a fire who's *alive* to *wonder* how you're getting on. That's *new*, for you too, I'd *wager.* Somebody *wondering.* *(a small smile)* Get used to it. I'm *stubborn* about the people who *bother.*

variants.text: *(Her eye cracks open, tracks you — old habit — and then, seeing it's you, the wariness drops and something the street never let her wear comes out instead: a grin, gap-toothed and *enormous.*)* *(she keeps her voice to a whisper, glancing at a small bundled shape asleep by the warm stones)* …He *drank* it. *(fierce, shining)* The grey-root brew. The Knotwife made it up proper and Wick *drank* it and the — the *rattle's* going *out* of his chest, you can *hear* it, it's *quieter* every — *(her voice cracks; she's nine and she hasn't slept properly in a week and her brother is going to *live*)* He *fussed* about the *taste.* *(a wet laugh)* He *fussed!* About the *taste!* Like a — like a *normal* little brother who's going to be *annoying* for *years!* *(she scrubs her face)* You *did* that. You went *past the last torch*, where *nobody* comes back from, for a *root*, for *Wick*, for *us.* I don't — I haven't got *words* big enough, and I've got *loads* of words, I *steal* the good ones. Sit *down.* You're — *(fierce, decided)* — you're *family* now. I *decided.* You don't get a *say.*

*(Her eye cracks open, tracks you — old habit, the hunted always wake — and then she relaxes, fractional, and shuffles upright against the bedroll.)* …Oh. *S'you.* *(she rubs her face)* Can't sleep proper. *(quietly)* Never could, on the street — you sleep with *one eye*, or you don't *keep* what's *yours.* *(she eyes the fire)* This is — *(she struggles for it)* — this is the *warmest* I've been in a *long* while. The *fire*, and the — *(she gestures vaguely at the camp, the safety of it)* — the *not-having-to-watch.* It's *strange.* My whole *self's* still braced for someone to *kick* me off the *good* spot. *(she squints at you)* You're not gonna *kick* me off the good spot. Are you. *(it's barely a question, but there's a whole life under it)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She pulls her knees up to her chin, small and — for the first time you've seen — not actively calculating an escape route, just sitting, by a fire, like a child.)* So. *(she watches the flames)* What's a *grown-up* do at a fire, when there's nothing to *steal* and nobody to *run* from? *(genuinely curious)* I've watched 'em from the *dark*, loads of times — the *fire* people, the *families.* Never knew what they *talked* about. Always wondered. *(she glances at you, shy)* …You can *show* me. If you want. I'm a *quick* study. Best on the *causeway.*

choices.text: "They don't *do* anything, Pip. That's the secret. They just sit, and they're warm, and they're together, and that's the whole of it. You don't have to earn the fire. You don't have to *watch* it. You just get to *have* it. Try it."
*(if her brother lives)* "How's Wick, really? Not the brave version — the true one. He drank the brew; what do the next years look like, for the two of you?"

[INSIGHT] "You're still keeping watch, Pip. Even here. Even safe. Your eyes haven't stopped doing the doorways. That's six years of nobody having your back — and it doesn't switch off because the fire's warm. It switches off because somebody *else* takes the watch. So: sleep. I've got it. I'll wake you if the dark so much as *breathes.*"

*(let your dead-touched sense settle over the sleeping camp)* "I can feel him from here, Pip — Wick. The little flame. All week I felt souls come *loose* from the world; his was one of them, guttering. It's not guttering now. It's *steady.* And yours — yours has stopped *bracing.* You're the first two souls I've felt take *root* in the world instead of slipping out of it. Sleep. You've both decided to *stay.*"
"Get some real sleep, Pip — both eyes. Nobody's kicking you off the good spot. Not tonight, not ever, not while I'm here."

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She's quiet a moment, taking that in — the cold thing telling her the warmest truth she's ever heard — and then she does something that costs a street-child everything: she *believes* it.)* …*Took root.* *(barely)* That's — nobody ever said me and Wick were *staying* anywhere. We *drift.* We *lift* and we *run* and we *don't get caught.* That's the whole — *(her voice wobbles)* — but you're saying we *stuck.* To the *world.* Like *fire* people. *(she scrubs her eyes, fierce)* You'd *know*, wouldn't you. You're the one who *came back.* You can *feel* who's *staying.* *(she lies back, and this time there's no bracing in it at all)* …Then we're *staying.* You *felt* it, so it's *true*, and I *trust* the cold thing more'n I trust my *own* scared eyes. *(a yawn, going under, safe)* …Wake me if it breathes. But I don't think it *will*, tonight. I think — *(barely)* — I think tonight the dark's on *our* side. First time. Account of *you.*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She frowns at the fire, working it like a sum that won't come out — and then, slowly, something in her small frame *unclenches*, a muscle she's held tight so long she'd forgotten it was hers to relax.)* …You don't have to *earn* it. *(she tests the words, suspicious)* That's — that's not how *anything* works. *Everything's* earned. Coppers, *spots*, *safety* — you *earn* it or you *go without*, that's the whole — *(she stops. Looks at the fire. Looks at you, not kicking her off the good spot.)* …huh. *(very quietly)* It's just — *warm.* And I'm just — *here.* And nobody's *counting* what I owe for it. *(her eyes are suddenly bright; she scrubs them angrily)* I'm not — *crying*, there's *smoke*, it's the *fire* — *(but she shuffles a little closer to it, and to you, and lets herself, for one whole minute, just be a warm kid by a fire)* …Is this what it's *like?* The *fire* people? *(barely)* …I could get *used* to this. That's — that's *dangerous*, getting *used* to it. *(a shaky breath)* …I'm gonna *risk* it.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She glances at the small sleeping shape, and her whole face goes soft in a way the market would never have survived showing.)* …The *true* version. *(quietly)* He's *weak* still. Won't run for a while, the Knotwife says — leg's still *wrong*, that won't ever *fix.* *(she picks at the bedroll)* But the *cough's* going. The *grey's* gone out of his *lips.* And he's — *(a small, wondering laugh)* — he's gone *bossy.* Been *dying* so long he forgot how to be a *little brother*, and now it's all coming *back* at once — telling me I *snore*, wanting a *story*, complaining the brew tastes like *pond.* *(she wipes her nose)* It's the *best* thing I ever *heard*, him being *annoying.* *(she looks at you)* I don't know what we *do* now. Never planned past *keeping him breathing.* No street, not with him *mending* — can't lift with a *little* one to mind. *(a breath, and then, carefully, like reaching for something too good to be real)* …the woman with the *sword* said there's *room.* On your *road.* For *useful* sorts. I'm the *quickest* hands on the *causeway*, and Wick's *clever*, cleverer'n *me*, and we don't eat *much*, and — *(fast, scared, hopeful)* — we could *come.* Couldn't we? We could be *yours.* The *useful* kind. Just — don't *leave* us in the *market.* I can't go *back* to the *crates.* Not now I know about *fires.*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(Her head comes up sharp — caught — and for a second the old wariness flares, defensive. And then it just… *crumbles*, because you've named the exact thing, and naming it is the first kindness anyone's done it.)* …I *can't* stop. *(small, frustrated, near tears)* I *know* it's *safe.* My *eyes* don't *believe* me. They keep doing the — the *doorways*, the *exits*, *who's-near-the-purse* — *(she presses her palms to them)* — six *years*, nobody *ever* had my back, you *sleep* and you *lose* — Wick, or the *haul*, or a *finger* — *(her voice wobbles)* — and you're telling me I can just — *hand it over?* The *watch?* To *you?* *(she stares at you, and the want and the terror of it are the same size)* …What if you *don't.* Wake me. What if you *sleep* too, and the dark — *(she stops, because you're already settling in, facing out, between her and the night)* …oh. *(very small)* You *meant* it. *(a long, shaking breath, and then — incredibly — she lies* down*, all the way down, and closes* both *eyes, and it is the most dangerous and trusting thing she has ever done)* …wake me if it *breathes.* You *promised.* *(barely)* …'night. First *both-eyes* sleep since — *(and she's out, gone, six years of exhaustion claiming its due, safe, watched, warm.)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She bristles and the watchfulness snaps back like a dropped portcullis.)* I'm *fine.* *(flat)* I *like* keeping watch. I'm *good* at it — *best* on the causeway, I *told* you. *(she hugs her knees)* Somebody's *gotta* do it. You lot'll all *conk* out and then where'll we *be* when the dark's got *teeth*, eh? *(but her eyes are red-rimmed, and the bravado's thin)* …I don't *need* tucking in. I've minded *myself* since I was *six* and minded *Wick* on *top.* *(quieter)* I'll sleep when I'm *dead*, and seeing as that's been *delayed* — *(a flicker of a real, exhausted look)* — I'll sleep when it's *light.* When I can *see* what's coming. *(she resettles, stubborn)* …Don't *fuss.* Fussing's for *fire* people. I'm still working up to it.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She studies your face a long moment — the whole hunted arithmetic of her, weighing it — and finds, this time, nothing in it to run from.)* …Not *ever.* *(she repeats it like she's filing it somewhere safe)* You *said* not *ever.* *(she lies back, slow, still half-braced)* I'm gonna *hold* you to that. I *remember* everything, me — every *promise*, every *lie*, who *kept* which. *(her eyes droop)* …You keep yours so far. *Two* for *two.* The crates, and the — *(a yawn swallows it)* …the *fire.* *(she's going under, fighting it from habit)* …if you *do* kick me off the good spot, I'll — I'll *nick* your *boots* — *whole* — laces *and* — *(and she's asleep, mid-threat, small and finally still, trusting you with the one thing she's never trusted anyone: her closed eyes.)*

variants.text: *(They look up from the little camp-sketch with a grin that has, since you took them on, almost entirely lost its manic edge — the look of a soul that finally has somewhere to put all that brightness.)* …The *road-walker* returns to the fire. *(they tap the sketch)* Sit, sit — I'm *mapping* you lot. Not the *dead* country tonight. *This* one. *(softly, the joy gone gentle)* You put my *name* on a *title cartouche*, friend, back at the wagon — well. *(they turn the sketch so you can see: the fire, the bedrolls, each soul labelled in the tiny deep-country hand, and beneath it all, in the corner where a surveyor signs, your name)* — I'm putting *yours* where I put *every* place that *mattered.* This is the *first* map I've ever drawn that I *want* to be *in*, instead of *measuring* from a half-mile *back.* Took me thirty years and a *Returned* to learn the *difference.*

*(They set the pen down as you approach, and for once they're not drawing the dead — they're watching the souls around the fire, with the soft attention of a cartographer who has found a country worth the looking.)* …Do you *see* them? *(quietly, nodding at the camp)* I've spent my whole *life* mapping where souls *go* — and I walk with *you* a *week* and learn there's a country I never *charted*, because I was always looking at the *far* shore: the country of the ones a *mercy* keeps *here.* *(they gesture at the bedrolls)* I can *read* it on them, the way I read the cold on the edges — *eased.* *Rooted.* Souls that were coming *loose* from the world, and *aren't*, because of choices only *you* made. *(a crooked smile)* I'm a *surveyor* of the *dead*, friend. You're the first soul I've met who *adds* to the *living* map. I'd *follow* you for *that* alone.

*(They glance up, and the brightness is wary tonight, the surveyor's eye taking a bearing on *you* for once.)* …Road-walker. *(carefully)* Sit. I'm — *mapping* the camp. *(a pause; they don't quite turn the sketch toward you)* You know, in *thirty* years of standing at the *edges*, I learned to read a *cold* on a soul — where it's *been*, what it's *paid.* And the cold on *you* tonight is — *(they choose the word)* — *colder* than the *Wall* put there. *(quietly)* You got things *done* this week. I *charted* the road you took to do them. Some of the *bearings* run through places I'd not have *gone.* *(they meet your eyes, honest, unafraid)* I'm *with* you, friend — I *threw* in, and a Sennet's *word* holds. But a *cartographer's* whole *use* is telling you, *plainly*, what *country* you're walking *into.* And the country *that* cold leads to — I've *surveyed* it. From *far* off. It's *cold* all the *way* down.

*(They're working by firelight when you come up — not the great dead-country chart, but a small careful sketch of the camp itself, each soul labelled in the tiny precise hand.)* …Ah. The *soul* I'm *following.* *(they grin, gesturing you down beside the stone they've made a drawing-table)* Sit. I'm doing something I've *never* done — mapping a place I'm *in*, instead of an edge I'm *measuring.* *(they tap the sketch)* The *fire.* The *bedrolls.* The *souls.* It's *terrible* work, frankly — I keep wanting to add *bearings* and *grid-lines* to a thing that doesn't *need* them — but I find I don't want to *lose* the bearings of *this*, either. *(a softer look)* A *dying* surveyor doesn't usually get a *campfire* to draw, friend. He gets an *edge*, and a *spyglass*, and a *year* of *cold* for his trouble. You've given me a *fire.* Ask the *mapmaker* anything. The *good* pens are *out.*

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(They sit cross-legged with the camp-sketch across their knees, pen behind one ear, more at ease than a soul who's been racing their own death has any right to be.)* So. *(a grin)* The *fire's* warm, the *good* country's *quiet*, and for *once* I'm not losing a *year* to a *bearing.* What's on the *road-walker's* mind?

choices.text: "What are you really drawing tonight, Sennet? That's not the country of the dead on your knee."
*(the hand Tibb told you to watch)* "How's the hand, Sennet? The exposure. Now you're not standing at the edges every other week — is the cold backing out of the joints, or is the damage done?"

*(one cold soul to another, by the fire)* "Here's the strange thing, Sennet. You've spent thirty years mapping the country I came back from — and I've spent this week watching you map the living from the edge of dying. We're the same survey run backwards. You measure the far shore from this side; I measure this side from the far one. Between us, we've nearly got the whole coastline."
"Have you sent word to Tibb? The lad counted your days for three years, Sennet. He should know the count just changed."
"Good mapping, Sennet. Get some rest — the road's there in the morning."

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(They turn the sketch toward you, almost shy about it, and it is — for a cartographer of *death* — startlingly *tender*: the fire at the centre, and each soul around it drawn small and exact and labelled.)* …No. *(quietly)* It's *you* lot. *(they point with the pen)* That's the *sellsword*, there, where the *dark* is — she sits *outside* the firelight even now, did you notice? Old habit. I drew her *half* in shadow, because that's *true.* That's the *apple-girl*, with the fruit she still won't *eat.* The *child*, who sleeps with one eye *open*, except — *(they squint)* — tonight, *both* shut, which I have *noted*, because it's *new.* *(they look up)* I cannot *help* it, friend. I'm a *surveyor.* I *fix* things by *drawing* them, so they can't be *lost.* And I have spent *thirty* years fixing the *places* the *dead* go — and not *once*, in all that time, did I think to *fix* a place I was *glad* to *be.* *(a crooked, wet-eyed smile)* This is the *most* important map I've ever made. It has *no* bearings on it at all. Just *who* was at the *fire*, the night the *cold* in me started to *back off.*

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(They flex the left hand — the one Tibb told you to watch — and this time, slowly, it opens almost all the way.)* …You *noticed.* *(quietly)* Of course you did; you *read* cold for a *living* now, same as me. *(they study the hand)* The *damage* that's *done* is *done* — four *fingers* that'll *ache* in the *wet* till I *die*, and a *slowness* in the *deep* of the joints the *warmth* won't *fix.* *(but there's wonder in it)* But the *creeping* — the *new* cold, the *year-a-survey* cold — that has *stopped*, friend. Because I'm not *standing* at the *edges* anymore. I'm *here*, at a *fire*, drawing the *deep* country from *your* reports instead of *freezing* it into my *own* bones a *bearing* at a time. *(they meet your eyes, and the manic mapmaker is, for a moment, just a tired soul who got handed back a fistful of years)* Tibb's *count* was *two.* Maybe *three.* *(barely)* It's not *two* anymore. I don't *know* the *new* number — I've *not* dared *take* the *bearing* — but it's *longer*, road-walker. It's *longer* because you *walked* up to my *wagon* and decided a *mad* mapmaker was worth *saving.* …I'll not *waste* the *extra.* Every *day* of it goes on the *map.* And *some* of it — *(a crooked grin)* — *some* of it, I think, I'll spend just *sitting* at *fires.* I'm *told* it's what the *living* do.

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(They go very still, the pen halting, and then a slow grin breaks over them — the grin of a surveyor handed the one bearing that closes a coastline.)* …*The same survey run backwards.* *(softly, delighted)* Oh, that's — *gods*, that's *exactly* it, isn't it. *(they sketch it without thinking, two arrows meeting)* I stand on the *warm* shore and *measure* the *cold* one, *paying* a *year* a *look.* You stand on the *cold* shore and *measure* the *warm* one, *carrying* a soul *out* each *trip.* *(they look up, ink-bright)* We're the *same* instrument, friend, pointed *opposite* ways — and a *bearing's* no *good* from *one* station, you need *two*, you need a *baseline* — *(they're laughing now, the real laugh)* — *that's* what we *are.* A *baseline.* The *first* two surveyors in the *history* of the *craft* to *triangulate* the *whole* coast of *death* from *both* sides at *once.* *(quieter, and there's something almost like awe)* The *atlas* won't be *mine*, road-walker. It can't be. It'll be *ours.* The *living* shore and the *dead* one, *measured* true, by the *one* soul who *goes* and the *one* who *came back.* …I waited my *whole* *life* for a *baseline.* I didn't know it'd walk up *wearing the cold* and *offer* me a *fire.*

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(The brightness gentles into something guiltier, and they rub the back of their neck with an ink-stained hand.)* …Tibb. *(quietly)* I *sent* word. First *thing.* A *runner*, from the last waystation, with a *letter* I — *(they wince)* — I *rewrote* four *times*, because *'I'm not dying on schedule anymore'* is a *cursedly* hard thing to say to a *lad* who's been *counting.* *(a breath)* I told him the *truth.* That a *Returned* walked up to the *wagon* and *changed* the *arithmetic.* That the *surveys* that were *killing* me — *you* take those *now*, the *deep*-country ones, and *come back*, and I *draw* them *warm.* That the *count* he kept, faithful, *terrible* — he can *stop* keeping it. *(their voice roughens)* …He wrote *back* one *line*, friend. *(they pull a creased scrap from the map-coat, and read it like scripture)* '*Then mind the new master better than you minded the old one, you impossible fool. I'll mind the wagon till you're home.*' *(they fold it away, eyes bright)* *Three* years the lad *counted* my *days.* And the *first* thing he does with a *longer* number is make me *promise* to *keep* it. …Mind me, road-walker. For *Tibb's* sake. He's *earned* an old fool who comes *home.*

nodes.speaker: Sennet
nodes.text: *(They give you an ink-stained salute and bend back over the little camp-sketch, adding, you suspect, one more careful label before they'll let themselves sleep.)* Good mapping to *you*, friend. *(a grin)* I'll *rest* — soon as I've *fixed* tonight to the *page*, so the *cold* can't take it. *(quietly, almost to the sketch)* Thirty years I drew the country the *living* *fear.* And here I am, *alive* past my *count*, drawing the country they *hope* for — a *fire*, and a *road*, and a few *souls* glad to *share* the *dark.* *(they dip the pen)* …Sleep well, road-walker. The *deep* country will *keep* till *morning.* It's *kept* this *long.* And now, for *once*, so will *I.*
